attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of intermittent pain in his legs. He describes spasms in his lower legs, which wake him from sleep and last for about five minutes. These symptoms are now occurring more frequently and disturbing his sleep. His medical history includes hypertension and alcohol-related liver disease, for which he takes amlodipine and spironolactone. He no longer drinks alcohol and smokes five cigarettes per day.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 136/74 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Neurological examination of the lower limbs is normal. There is no evidence of varicose veins. Femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are present bilaterally. There is no peripheral oedema.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is most likely to provide effective symptomatic relief?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic symptoms of nocturnal leg cramps - painful muscle spasms occurring during sleep that typically affect the calf muscles. These are common in older adults and can significantly impact quality of life through sleep disruption.

Vitamin B complex supplementation has been shown to be effective in reducing the frequency and severity of nocturnal leg cramps, with studies showing improvement in up to 80% of patients. The exact mechanism is not fully understood but may relate to the role of B vitamins in neuromuscular function.

Quinine, while historically used and effective for nocturnal leg cramps, is no longer recommended as first-line therapy due to the risk of serious adverse effects including cardiac arrhythmias, thrombocytopenia, and cinchonism (tinnitus, headache, and visual disturbances).

Diltiazem, a calcium channel blocker, has shown modest benefit in some studies by reducing cramp frequency, but evidence is limited and it does not affect cramp intensity or duration. Given this patient is already on amlodipine, adding another calcium channel blocker would not be appropriate.

Magnesium supplementation has shown benefit primarily in pregnancy-related leg cramps but has not demonstrated consistent effectiveness in other populations. While it is sometimes used empirically, evidence for its use in idiopathic nocturnal leg cramps is lacking.

Bedtime calf stretching exercises, while commonly recommended as a preventive measure, have limited evidence for effectiveness as a sole intervention. They may be suggested as part of a comprehensive management plan but are unlikely to provide adequate relief on their own.
learningPoints: Nocturnal leg cramps are common in older adults and can significantly impact sleep quality
Vitamin B complex supplementation is effective first-line treatment for nocturnal leg cramps
Quinine is no longer recommended for leg cramps due to risk of serious adverse effects
references: Hallegraeff J, de Greef M, Krijnen W, et al. Criteria in diagnosing nocturnal leg cramps: a systematic review. Criteria in diagnosing nocturnal leg cramps: a systematic review. BMC Family Practice (2017) 18:29
Herzberg J, Stevermer J. Treatments for Nocturnal Leg Cramps. Am Fam Phys. 2017; 96:496B.
